(source: most recent 10-K from SEC)

Revenue doesn't always spike every single year with Royal Gold, but that operating margin, which was already extremely high, is only getting better as new projects they have rights to grow and they add some excellent deals. Some of those investments are for streams and royalties that are still in the process of ramping up too, so we saw growth in 2024 along with improved margins, a trend which may well continue into 2025 and beyond.

2024 was a very positive year for Royal Gold. The conference call reported revenue up 19%, operating cash flow up 27%, and new income up 39%. They also reported an adjusted EBITDA margin of 81% for the full year, up to 84% in the fourth quarter. We saw them narrowly beat earnings estimates, the fourth straight year they've done so, and the fifth straight year they beat on revenue estimates.

Royal Gold isn't exactly screamingly cheap here, with a P/E of 35.63, quite a premium to the sector median of 18.63. I believe that's potentially misleading though because Royal Gold isn't exactly like the rest of the sector. Dealing with streams and royalties instead of digging in the ground themselves means they don't have to take on certain risks, and there's a case to be made that the premium is justified.

This wasn't even an optimum year, though a very good one. The Carmen de Andacollo property we talked about before had some problems because of drought conditions. Production there, and by extension the gold available to Royal Gold, is expected to be higher in 2025 and through 2027.

What About 2025 and Beyond?

Estimate-wise, things are only looking better going forward. In 2025, the expectation is revenues growing to $850.68 million and EPS up to $6.55. In 2026, that grows to $996.39 million and $7.59 per share, respectively. Once again, those forward P/Es are higher than what's estimated for the sector, reflective of the premium Royal Gold is going for because of their somewhat different type of business.

There's other stuff we might look forward to in 2025 and beyond. As mentioned, Royal Gold has 1.7 million deferred ounces of silver coming to them from Pueblo Viejo. There's no telling if that will happen this year, but if it does that could be substantial. Right now, the stream is at 30% spot price on silver, which means they'd make 70% of that price on 1.7 million ounces, that's $54 million in profit at current prices, which would be a nice boost.

Production at Cortez is expanding quite a bit, and it is anticipated that Royal Gold will be getting even more royalties out of that site than they did in years past. That's particularly true of the "crossroads" area of Cortez, where their royalty is highest at around 9.4%.

Royal Gold also isn't done acquiring new deals when the opportunity is there. In December, they acquired a 2.5% net smelter royalty at Cactus, a copper project in Arizona. They spent $55 million on that. This clearly won't be one of their major properties like the principal properties we talked about earlier, but every little acquisition means more income. That's where things get interesting.

Balance Sheet and Dividend Growth

Royal Gold pays only 45¢ quarterly for a dividend right now, which is only about a 1% yield in a sector where the median is 2.29%. That's not to say their dividend isn't something to talk about. That's because Royal Gold has annually increased the dividend for 24 consecutive years. If dividend growth is your thing, there is very much reason to be optimistic here. Now let's talk about the balance sheet.

Cash and Equivalents

$195.5 million

Total Current Assets

$275.3 million

Stream and Royalty Interests

$3.04 billion

Total Current Liabilities

$85.2 million

Total Shareholder Equity

$3.13 billion

(source: most recent 10-K from SEC)

This is a very strong balance sheet, obviously, but I bring it up to point out the conspicuous lack of debt. Royal Gold had a revolving credit facility, but they totally paid it off in 2024, so now they're totally free and clear, and have a decent amount of cash on hand to be able to either raise the dividend further, or acquire more streams and royalties if the opportunity presents. Honestly, doing both seems likely, given their track record.

Risks: Not Totally Disconnected From Gold Prices

I think Royal Gold has made much of being effectively inflation-proof, a function of their revenue coming from the price of minerals that would be expected to rise particularly in inflationary environments. That's not to say they can totally not care about the price of the minerals, though, especially gold.

Their income comes in two forms, streams where they buy those minerals at a discount, and royalties where they get a percentage of the smelter revenue. In both cases if the minerals in question drop in price, those percentages they make in profit also add up to less.

Think of it this way. If they get gold at 30% of spot, they make 70%. If spot price is $3,200, like it is today, that translates to $2,240 per ounce in profit for Royal Gold. If gold prices dropped to $2,000, they'd still make 70%, but that would only be $1,400 per ounce. They're always going to make a profit, but in a gold bear market that profit would be smaller. Given the premium the stock trades at, we could reasonably expect Royal Gold's stock to decline in a gold bear market as well.

Now in this environment, I don't really expect gold prices to soften any time soon. If anything, it seems like a lot of people are favoring precious metals as a safety asset during economic uncertainty, as they historically have.

Beyond that, there's also a risk if the properties they have streams or royalties in don't produce as expected. Royal Gold is pretty good at picking the right sites, run by the right companies, but as we saw in the past couple of years at Carmen de Andacollo, even situations like droughts can harm production and temporarily decrease how much stream they have access to. Elsewhere, where they have stream interest in the gold more than the copper, a mine hitting an unusual copper-rich seam will also mean temporarily less gold produced.

A Flight to Safety, But an Even Better Entry Point Would Be Nice

So at the end of the day, I believe Royal Gold is a good fit for a flight to safety investment. The company pretty much can't help but make money with this business model, and given that economic uncertainty is likely bullish for gold prices, we can expect even better performances in bad times.

I'm rating this a buy, but withholding strong buy at these prices because it's trading at what is effectively an all-time high. Again, there are reasons for that, but a cheaper entry point wouldn't be unwelcome if there's a sudden shift in market sentiment. Buying right now absolutely makes sense for some, though, especially if your portfolio lacks any precious metal exposure. There's a premium, but in my view it's totally justified.

Investors who do buy should keep an eye on the price of gold, obviously, but maybe also keep track of how those principal properties we were talking about are doing. Especially encouraging would be if the Pueblo Viejo mine starts producing more silver and can finally deliver those deferred ounces because that would be another big shot in the arm for Royal Gold, which is already flying high by all indicators.

data.title: Ford: Rapid Downward EPS Revisions Can Become A Self-Fulfilling Prophecy
data.description:
Ford (NYSE:F) (NEOE:F:CA) stock has seen massive swings in the last few weeks due to a new direction in the tariff situation. The dividend yield is now over 6.5%, which might be attractive for some investors. However, it should be noted that many top financial executives like BlackRock’s Larry Fink, JPM’s Jamie Dimon, and others have warned of a recession or an economic slowdown in the near future. Ford is a highly cyclical stock, and it has never performed well during an economic slowdown. During the Great Recession and the dot-com bubble burst, Ford’s stock showed 70%-80% decline. Over 50% of Ford’s deliveries are in international markets. The supply chain is also dependent on trade with different regions. Even a modest increase in tariffs could lead to a major decline in profitability for the company. In the previous article, it was mentioned that we could see headwinds for the dividend in the near term.

Some of the bullish analysts have pointed to the “Trump put” effect during the first term, which means that the White House will make sure there is not a major recession because of the impact it can have on the stock market and bond market. However, even a minor slowdown in the economy could have a big impact on Ford. This is reflected in the wide range of forward EPS projections. Ford stock is trading at a modest forward PE multiple of 5.8 for fiscal year ending Dec 2027, but the lowest EPS estimate is $0.85, and the highest is almost 3X at $2.3. Most of these estimates were prior to the recent tariff war. It is likely that further divergence in forward EPS will be seen in the future. This uncertainty adds massive risk to the stock. I don’t believe the downside risk is adequately priced in, and we could see further correction in the stock over the next few months as the tariff situation evolves.

Rapid downward EPS revisions

In the near term, getting a lot of downward EPS revisions can hurt the sentiment for a stock. Ford has seen 19 down revisions in the last 3 months compared with only 1 up revision. This will continue to hurt the sentiment towards the stock over the next few quarters.

Figure: EPS revisions of Ford. Source: Seeking Alpha

Figure: Analysts rating of Ford. Source: Seeking Alpha

The tariff situation is particularly risky for Ford because it has a very diverse supply chain. It delivered 2.2 million vehicles in US in 2024 out of a total of 4.47 million. Hence, over half of the vehicles for Ford are sold in international markets. Any tit-for-tat in the tariff war will inevitably hurt the profit margin of Ford. Even a minor dip in gross margin can have a huge impact on the EPS.

Figure: Units sold in different regions. Source: Company Filings

Slowdown in the economy

Ford is a cyclical stock, and it has never done well during economic slowdowns. During the prior recessions, including the early 90s, the dot-com bust, and The Great Recession, Ford stock saw a significant correction of 70%-80%. It is highly likely that if there is an economic slowdown in the next few quarters, Ford will face a big headwind. Many top financial executives have started warning about a decline in consumer confidence and have also mentioned that a few companies are limiting their capex. This can become a self-fulfilling prophecy, and a higher tariff environment can cause stagflation.

It will be important to hear from the tech executives in the upcoming earnings call about their capex forecast. Amazon (AMZN) has earlier increased its capex forecast to $100 billion, and Alphabet (GOOG) has announced capex of $75 billion. These are massive numbers, and any cut in the capex could have a negative impact on the GDP growth numbers. Similarly, other big tech companies have also announced huge capex plans. If they start pulling back from these big investments, we might see a strong sign of a slowdown in the economy.

Ford has a high dividend yield of 6.5%, which is one of the key reasons why many investors are attracted to the stock. But any slowdown or recession will immediately force Ford’s management to reduce the dividend and conserve cash. Investors looking for higher yields might look for more defensive telecom stocks during these uncertain economic times.

“Trump put” and an uncertain EPS projection

Analysts have mentioned the “Trump put” effect during the first term. This means that any major policy decision by the White House will ensure that the stock and bond markets are not adversely affected. However, even during the first term, Ford stock saw a decline of over 30% between 2017 and the end of 2019. It is also unlikely that the tariff rates will go back to previous levels for all countries. Even a few percentage point increase in tariff can have a big impact on Ford’s wafer-thin margins.

We can see from the above chart that Ford has a much lower gross margin than GM (GM) or BYD (OTCPK:BYDDY). Even a few percentage point erosion of gross margin due to tariffs can have a big impact on the EPS in the next few quarters.

The stock price might look attractive from a forward PE metric. However, we should also look at the massive debt pile of the company. It is also important to look at the huge variation in forward EPS projections for a mature company like Ford.

Figure: Forward EPS projection of Ford. Source: Seeking Alpha

For the fiscal year ending Dec 2026, analysts expect EPS to range between $1.06 and $1.86. For the fiscal year ending Dec 2027, analysts expect EPS to range between $0.85 and $2.30. Hence, the highest EPS estimate is 2.7 times the lowest EPS estimate. Many of these estimates were prior to the tariff situation. It is likely that over the next few weeks, we could see even a greater divergence in forward EPS estimates. This increases the risk for investors and hurts the sentiment towards the stock.

Investor Takeaway

Ford stock is seeing rapid downward EPS revisions in the last few weeks. There is also growing warning from many financial executives about the possibility of a slowdown or a recession. Ford stock does not perform well during an economic slowdown. It has shown a correction of 70%-80% in the past few major recessions.

Even a modest increase in tariff rate could hurt the supply chain of Ford. If the company is not able to pass this extra cost to customers, we could see further erosion of its margins. The forward PE multiple of Ford is quite low, and it has an attractive dividend yield of 6.5%. But we should also look at the massive debt pile. The forward EPS estimates have a huge variation, which reflects the lack of certainty in forward earnings. This increases the risk for investors, and we could see further correction in the stock to price in these headwinds.

data.title: Rubrik: A Bigger Correction May Be On The Way (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Is hyper growth still in vogue? Only a few months ago, the S&P 500 had been roaring to brand-new heights, fueled by optimism over hopes for President Trump’s pro-business policies, deregulation, and an extension of 2017 era tax cuts. Now, the narrative has shifted, with new recession headlines out every day. The question for investors now is: is it still a ripe time to invest in growth stocks, or is it time to dial back.

I’ve been happily snapping up shares of fallen former market darlings, but a key requirement for my portfolio is “growth at a reasonable price.” Cheap stocks only. Even companies with blistering growth rates, especially those that are small-caps and have a shorter track record for execution, can see their growth trajectories turn on a dime during a recession. And with that backdrop in mind, in taking a fresh look at Rubrik (NYSE:RBRK), I’ve soured on the stock recently. Rubrik is down ~20% from YTD highs, but the stock has still leapt 60% over the past year: gains which, I think, are very vulnerable as the company undergoes a natural deceleration curve.

I last wrote a buy opinion on Rubrik in December, when the stock was still trading at $64 per share. Since then, Rubrik has only fallen modestly, at least relative to other high-growth software stocks. Its outlook for FY25 is calling for a steep drop-off in growth rates, and with more conservatism pervading in the markets today, the company’s losses may attract further attention. With this in mind, I’m dropping my rating on Rubrik to neutral, and I am encouraging investors to slowly lock in gains on this position (even as it’s falling) to make room for cheaper growth stocks.

At current share prices, I see more of a balanced bull versus bear case for Rubrik. On the bright side for the company:

  • Big data is the basic foundation of AI and large language models. Businesses are increasingly looking to integrate AI automation into their everyday business processes, which also means that resilience - or making sure that a backup exists in case data and systems go dark and are under cyberattack - is also a key necessity. While many companies focus on cybersecurity, fewer share Rubrik’s specializing in resilience.
  • Rubrik addresses a large $53 billion TAM, with a multi-year growth trajectory fueled by rising AI-related use cases.

At the same time, however, new risks have emerged that, in my view, balance out the positives:

  • A recession complicates already-tightening IT budgets. Smaller single-product software companies may have a tougher time selling in this environment, as budget scrutiny increases and pulls the plug on many deals. This is especially true for a company like Rubrik that lies on expansion deals (customers spending more on the platform via higher consumption) for a large chunk of its growth. Rubrik may not suffer from any direct first-order impacts from tariffs, but declining business confidence and greater budget economization will certainly take its toll.
  • Red ink makes Rubrik stand out. Though Rubrik is a growth star, with growth rates north of 40% in FY24, its double-digit operating loss margins may scare some investors off in a more conservative market environment. Losses are natural for an earlier stage high-growth company, but that doesn’t mean investors will be looking the other way on the red ink during a recession.

What additionally sidelines me on Rubrik is its persistently high valuation multiples. At current share prices near $60, Rubrik trades at a market cap of $11.69 billion. After we net off the $705.1 million of cash and $322.3 million of debt on Rubrik’s most recent balance sheet, the company’s resulting enterprise value is $11.31 billion.

Meanwhile, for FY26 (the year for Rubrik ending in January 2026), Rubrik has guided to $1.145-$1.161 billion in revenue, which represents a growth range of 29-31% y/y. To be sure, Rubrik still classifies as a high-growth company. But that growth rate represents 17 points of deceleration at the midpoint relative to 47% growth in the most recent Q4. Rubrik also fails at the “Rule of 40” distinction (growth plus pro forma operating margins), thanks to its operating losses.

Rubrik’s valuation sits at 9.8x EV/FY26 revenue. Now, that’s not bad for a ~30% growth stock - but during a bull market. The bellwether stocks in the software industry, like Salesforce (CRM) and Workday (WDAY), have fallen to a ~6x revenue multiple. And while Rubrik’s growth certainly affords it some room for premium (hence why I’m neutral, and not bearish on the stock), I don’t see much room for multiples expansion either, especially because as a single-product company with limited use cases, Rubrik’s sales momentum has further risk than a broader multi-product platform like Salesforce during a downturn.

All in all, it’s no longer worth hanging on to this stock. Begin to divest this name and use the proceeds for other discounted growth stocks.

Q4 download

This being said, for now, Rubrik has continued to exhibit strong trends. Let’s now go through Rubrik’s latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

Rubrik’s revenue grew 47% y/y to $258.1 million, beating Wall Street’s expectations of $233.1 million (+33% y/y) by a significant margin, while also accelerating from 43% y/y growth in Q3. The magnitude of the company’s beat and its track record for issuing conservative guidance does give us a glimmer of hope that its outlook calling for major deceleration in FY26 may be tilted on the conservative side to allow room for a smooth “beat and raise” pattern this year.

Rubrik has also continued to add ARR at a healthy pace. The company added $91 million of net-new ARR in the quarter, up 39% y/y, and well above the $83 million it added in Q3 and just $59 million in the prior-year Q4. The fact that Rubrik's current ARR covers 95% of its revenue guidance for FY25 gives us more confidence in the company's ability to hit its targets.

Of course, that outlook was before the onset of tariffs and the macroeconomic shocks that followed. Rubrik is a failsafe and an additional outlay on top of traditional cybersecurity products. Amid companies' push for budget economization in a tougher economy, it may see new business deals dry up and expansion trends slow.

We note that there may be added volatility this year from Rubrik's own shift in its sales compensation package, from a semi-annual incentive comp to annual. This means fewer salespeople will be pushing to close deals in the first half of FY25, which stacks on top of already-difficult macro conditions. The company is expecting, as a result of its incentive shift, that Rubrik will close more deals and increase ARR more in the second half than the first half. Per CFO Kiran Choudary's remarks on the Q4 earnings call:

As a reminder, we run our business on annual net new subscription ARR, but I’d like to give some guideposts on our current perspective on quarterly seasonality. We continue to expect higher net new subscription ARR in the second half of the year versus the first half. While this is relatively in line with normal seasonality, it is also reflective of a shift to annual sales comp plans this year versus semiannual previously. Currently, we expect the first half to comprise approximately 40% of the full year net new subscription ARR with Q1 specifically contributing approximately 24% of full year net new subscription ARR."

Rubrik has also made meaningful leaps in profitability, with pro forma operating margins rising more than 30 points y/y to -11%.

The company also barely broke past breakeven on a free cash flow basis, with FCF of $21.6 million in FY25 hitting a 2% margin versus a -4% loss in FY24. Still, investors' standards for profitability are higher in a risky macro environment, and Rubrik is still signaling negative EPS in FY26.

Key takeaways

While I remain convinced that Rubrik is a strong company in the long term, I think sentiment will continue to shift against this stock as growth is expected to slow in the first half of FY26, due to macro pressures plus Rubrik's sales incentive plan change. With Rubrik one of the few mid-cap enterprise software companies to maintain a roughly double-digit revenue multiple, I think there's more room on the downside in Rubrik's correction. In my view, it's time to move to the sidelines on this stock.

data.title: Hercules Capital: You Can Lock In A 10% Yield Again
data.description:
High-yielding business development companies like Hercules Capital, Inc. (NYSE:HTGC) have gotten clobbered in April, a consequence of a surge in market volatility that traces back to new tariffs the Trump Administration imposed last week.

The increase in volatility, in my view, opens up a buying window for passive income investors to load up on BDCs that are of the highest quality, even if they are still selling for a comparatively high NAV multiple.

In my last piece on Hercules Capital, I pointed to the business development company’s long history of growing its portfolio and income as well as the fact that the BDC was profiting from record gross originations in 2024.

In my view, Hercules Capital offers passive income investors a rock-solid dividend, and they can now, thanks to the carnage happening in the stock market, lock in a 10% dividend yield.

Hercules Capital Has A Growing Portfolio, Record Originations And Could Profit From Strong Demand For New Venture Capital Deals In 2025

Hercules Capital has positioned itself has a leading capital provider to technology companies which has led the business development company to build tight relationships in the sector. This relationship network has filled the BDC’s investment pipeline and led to a steady stream of new deals in 2024.

As a matter of fact, 2024 was the most successful year ever for the business development company in terms of gross fundings: Last year, Hercules Capital had total gross fundings of $1.81 billion, a record in the BDC’s history and reflecting 13% YoY growth.

Thanks to this steady stream of new deals, Hercules Capital profited from a 7% boost to its total investment income (fueled by payment-in-kind income) and net investment income. Both Hercules Capital’s total investment income and net investment income were the highest amounts ever realized.

Due to new investment fundings, Hercules Capital’s debt portfolio grew to its biggest amount as well: $3.5 billion, up 14% YoY. Though the business development company’s portfolio yields slid lower in the past year, thanks to pressure on short-term interest rates, Hercules Capital still produces competitive yields (13.7% in 4Q24) that are very close to the BDC’s long-term median of 14.1%.

The outlook for additional rate cuts is probably slightly negative right now, as the central bank might contemplate lowering short-term interest rates in order to react to last week’s market panic.

Taking into account that Hercules Capital has one of the best LTM dividend pay-out ratios in the peer group, I think that the BDC makes a compelling proposition to passive income investors.

Hercules Capital is primarily focused on venture capital-backed technology companies, which unites the BDC’s fortune with in growth prospects of the VC market. Based on Sapphire Ventures data, global venture capital deals rebounded in 2024, reflecting 7% YoY growth and led to a full-year annual deal volume of $371 billion.

The growth backdrop for 2025 also appears to be quite positive, in my opinion, with certain sectors like genAI specifically poised to drive deal activity. In 2024, genAI deals skyrocketed to $58.6 billion and with AI poised to being a growth catalyst for many companies across many different industries, this particular segment could turn out to represent an upside catalyst for deal-making activity in 2025.

Since Hercules Capital has a focus on originations in the technology sector, the BDC might be a winner if more genAI deals are requiring new venture debt.

Where Does HTGC Stand In Terms Of Dividend Coverage To BDC Peers?

Hercules Capital has a solid long-term history of covering its dividend with net investment income. As a matter of fact, because of Hercules Capital’s strong dividend coverage, the business development company has been able to pay supplemental dividends for eighteen straight quarters.

In the fourth quarter, Hercules Capital earned $0.49 per share in net investment income and paid out $0.40 per share in base dividend (plus another $0.07 per share in supplemental dividends). This led us to a dividend pay-out ratio, based on net investment income, of 82% in 4Q24.

Hercules Capital, as we will see in the valuation section, is selling for one of the highest net asset value premiums in the BDC peer group and this is directly related to the business development company having one of the lowest LTM, NII-based dividend pay-out ratios and having a long history of paying supplement dividends. Hercules Capital’s LTM dividend pay-out ratio stands at 80% compared to a BDC peer group average of 87%.

Passive Income Investors Can Lock In A 10% Yield Again

The market selloff has led to a considerable multiple compression for the stock price of Hercules Capital. As a matter of fact, it was not unusual just a short while ago to see the stock of the business development company change hands for a more than 70% premium to net asset value (which as of December 31, 2024 sat at $11.66 per share).

Still, as of April 11, 2025, Hercules Capital is still the most expensive business development company in the peer group, with a Price-To-NAV ratio of 1.43x.

Every other BDC is cheaper based on net asset value, including my two favorites in terms of pay-out stability: Ares Capital (ARCC) and Blackstone Secured Lending Fund (BXSL). The peer group average Price-To-NAV ratio is 0.95x, which makes HTGC about 50% more expensive than the average BDC.

Once the market regains its confidence and bilateral trade deals coming negotiated, I think that Hercules Capital will re-rate to its valuation of 1.7x net asset value, which places the intrinsic value for the business development company stock at $20. In addition, Passive income investors get to collect a 10% dividend yield, not counting the supplemental dividends that the BDC will pay in 2025.

Why The Investment Thesis Might Not Play Out

A key variable for the investment thesis is whether or not Hercules Capital will be able to defend high portfolio yields and its low dividend pay-out ratio. The central bank could throw a wrench into the investment thesis if it decides to lower short-term interest rates in order to calm markets and support asset prices.

Moreover, Hercules Capital is 97.4% floating-rate positioned, meaning a decrease in interest rates is probably going to affect Hercules Capital’s net investment income in a negative way.

That said, taking into account HTGC’s better-than-average dividend pay-out ratio, the BDC should be able to sustain its dividend pay-out of $0.40 per share per quarter in 2025.

My Conclusion

The tariff tantrum has created a new set of investment opportunities, inside and outside the high-yield market. Investors that concentrate predominantly on income-producing investment opportunities may want to consider Hercules Capital as a ‘Buy’ on the dip.

The business development company has consistently covered its dividend with net investment income and has also fairly regularly paid supplementary dividends on a quarterly basis in order to distribute portfolio excess income which obviously helps passive income investors to boost their total returns.

Though Hercules Capital is the most expensive BDC that investors can add to their passive income portfolios, I think that the high premium to net asset value is justified taking into account that Hercules Capital has a growth catalyst in 2025 (higher deal activity in the VC market) and a very high margin of safety.

data.title: Elevance Health: A Hidden Gem Amongst Healthcare Turbulence
data.description:
In a health insurance marketplace that has offered nothing but the stresses of unpredictable rates, rising costs and unprecedentedly high utilization, Elevance Health (NYSE:ELV) has demonstrated incredible resilience. As other health insurers' earnings results plunged to cyclical lows in 2024, Elevance's results remained steady due to its unique combination of a predominantly fee-based Commercial business, industry-leading preventive care initiatives and disciplined deployment of capital. While the company's execution was not perfect, especially in its management of the Medicare and Medicaid membership populations, shortfalls in these Regulatory business areas are likely to improve in the coming years and further fuel membership and earnings growth that should allow prudent investors in the company to realize outsized returns.

A Commercial Success Story

One of the key components of Elevance Health's fiscal durability during the past few years has been the higher than average proportion of the firm's membership enrolled in fee-based Commercial plans rather than risk-based plans. Fee-based plans, or administrative services only plans, allow employer groups to leverage the health plan's claim processing and provider network apparatuses to act as their own mini-insurer by taking on the financial risk for their employees' healthcare. The upside of this approach is that the employer pays much lower fees to the health insurance company as well as the cost of any claims incurred by their employees over the course of the year. When a company's employees are mostly younger and/or healthier than average, this can be extremely advantageous. However, in an environment where medical costs and utilization are scaling upwards as they have in recent years, the main beneficiary of this arrangement is the health insurance company as the risk inherent in these trends is pushed off to its customers. With 27.2 million fee-based Commercial members, representing just under 60% of the company's total membership, Elevance Health has proven to be extremely well insulated from the unpredictable cost pressures of the health insurance marketplace.

With the majority of the risk of health expenditures borne by its customers, Elevance has had the opportunity to focus on and invest in programs that facilitate preventive care and prescription adherence, providing value-added benefits for participating employer groups and enhancing retention. The most prominent example of this is the company's Total Health, Total You advocacy program, which CEO Gail Boudreaux reported had reduced inpatient admissions, the most expensive drivers of medical cost aside from pharmaceuticals, by roughly 7% year over year. By providing this framework for success to its clients, Elevance has been able to continue to grow fee-based membership and deploy capital to enhance shareholder returns instead of paying medical claims. While the dividend yield is modest at 1.5%, the company continues to buy back shares and reduce its outstanding share count year after year, with a reduction from 235.9 million shares in 2023 to 231.7 million shares in 2024 and a remaining share repurchase authorization of $9.3 billion as of January 2025.

Regulatory Challenges

For all of its success on the Commercial side of its business, Elevance Health has experienced substantial challenges in its Regulatory business, including both Medicare and Medicaid. On the Medicare side, Elevance fell just short of a CMS Star rating of 4 stars for 2024, missing out on bonus payments totaling $375 million due to what they allege is an unfair rounding error whereby a raw score of 3.749656 rounds down to 3.5 stars when 3.75 would have rounded up to 4 stars. As CMS Star rating cut points are scheduled to become even stricter beginning in 2025, this may serve as a continued headwind unless Elevance's Medicare member health outcomes and reported service quality measures improve substantially moving forward. Compounding the company's Regulatory weakness, Medicaid membership declined from 10.5 million in 2023 to 8.9 million in 2024 due to the scheduled removal of COVID-era expanded income thresholds, and the company unfortunately failed to recapture much of the lost membership in its existing subsidized ACA Commercial plans.

Valuation

Elevance Health currently trades at ~$440 per share, with a non-GAAP price/earnings ratio of just under 13x on a forward basis. This is substantially lower than the sector median of a bit over 16x, and despite a D- valuation grade on Seeking Alpha I believe this represents a substantial upside opportunity as the only metric weighing the grade down seems to be the PEG value during past 12 months. With the resiliency of the company's Commercial business and protection from downside risk outlined above, as well as a further projected share count reduction to 225-226 million in 2025, earnings are poised to resume growth despite continued sector challenges. As such, multiple expansion to the sector median or a price of $552 per share assuming the 2025 midpoint of $34.50 adjusted earnings per share seems entirely reasonable and achievable, representing upside of ~25%.

Risks

The greatest risk to the Elevance Health investment thesis is continued weakness in the Regulatory business, much of which is outside of the company's control. For example, Elevance is subject to the whims of CMS and state authorities when it comes to Medicare and Medicare reimbursement rates and the cut points utilized to determine CMS Star ratings. With that said, if Elevance is able to leverage its strengths such as value-added benefits and the strength of its Carelon Rx and Services segments in vertically integrating costs, which has been highly successful on the Commercial side, this may help to mitigate continued escalation of utilization, especially among the Medicaid population.

Conclusion

Elevance Health has shown itself to be a port in the storm during what has proven to be a generationally volatile marketplace for health insurance in the post-COVID world. Despite rising costs and utilization, Elevance's earnings results remained flat in 2024 and are projected to begin growing again in 2025, thanks largely to solid management, a diverse portfolio and excellent cost discipline. With equity investments in turmoil due to tariff risks, a flight to the safety of healthcare stocks makes sense, and of those few are as well positioned to succeed as Elevance Health. As such, I rate Elevance as a BUY at current prices.

data.title: MongoDB: You May Regret Buying The Dip (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
Intro

Back in mid February, I slapped a sell rating on MongoDB, Inc. (NASDAQ:MDB) and since then the stock has plummeted nearly 45%. I believe it continues to be a sell. In the below analysis, I determine that the majority of technicals indicate further pain for the stock. The few positives are unable to convince me that the bottom is in for MDB. For the fundamentals, Q4 earnings were mixed but sluggish revenue growth and declining cash flow from operations are worrying. Furthermore, Q1 guidance shows that revenue growth will decline to an even lower level. The P/S ratio seems to show an overreaction to the poor guidance figures as there has been major contraction. However, with its premium above the IT sector being over 140%, there are reasons to believe that the stock has been chronically overvalued. Therefore, I reiterate my sell rating on MDB on the basis of continued technical weakness and unattractive fundamentals.

Technical Analysis

The Basics

Trends, Support & Resistance

Currently, MDB is in both a near and longer-term downtrend. The longer-term downtrend line began back in early December, but is quite distant from the stock currently. The near term downtrend began in mid-February, and it has been acting as resistance on the previous two trading days. There are three additional resistance levels that I have identified above. The nearest one to the stock is at around 175. This level was support for a brief period at the end of March and seems to have contributed to resistance just two days ago. The next resistance level sits at around 205. Two March rally peaks ended at this level, and so it seems to have acted as a ceiling for a short while. Lastly, there is distant resistance at around 255. That level was a major downside gap that occurred on overwhelming volume. As for support, there isn't a major level in this timeframe, but the bullish engulfing pattern that I have circled shows the potential of a bullish reversal and could act as some support for the stock moving forward.

Moving Averages & Indicators

The 50-day SMA has been below the 200-day SMA for the past six months, and so clearly sentiment has been bearish for the longer run. After the gap between the SMAs narrowed into the start of March, it has been expanding again to show a resurgence in bearish momentum. For the EMAs, the 9 day EMA broke below the 21 day EMA for a bearish crossover back in late February to reflect near term bearishness. The gap between the EMAs has since expanded but is currently holding quite steady, and so at least near term bearish momentum isn't accelerating at this point.

The stock broke below the Bollinger Bands midline back in late February as a sign that a nearer term downtrend could be forming. It was also at signal that the long-term downtrend remained strong. The stock found resistance at the midline days after the break below, confirming that sentiment had shifted bearish. For the MACD, the indicator and its signal line have been quite flat for the past few weeks, and so there has been uncertainty and indecision in the stock. Note that the MACD has shown some positive divergence as the indicator has held above the lows seen in mid-March despite the stock continuing to drop. For the RSI, it currently has a reading of 34.97 after bouncing back from a below 30 level. This shows that the bears remain in strong control overall. Unlike the MACD, the RSI confirms the latest plunge in the stock, as it made a six-month low recently. Lastly, for the stochastics, there was a bullish crossover recently as the %K rallied above the %D. In addition, the %K has broken back above 20, showing that near term bearish pressure may be receding now.

Takeaway

In my view, it is clear that most technicals remain bearish for MDB despite some signs of strength. The chart shows that the stock remains in both a near and long-term downtrend, the moving averages show that bearish momentum remains strong, while there are some mixed signs in the indicators. The MACD signals near term uncertainty, while the RSI and stochastics show a modest retreat by the bears. For the long term, the MACD displays bullish divergence while the RSI confirms the recent plunge to six month lows. Overall, continued technical weakness is expected but keep your eyes peeled for more bottoming signals that may occur in the future.

Fibonacci

Time Zones

Using the early December high as the starting date, MDB seems to have reacted to the previous two Fibonacci Time Zone days. Just shortly after the 34th day, the stock began a major bearish reversal after the rally from late December to mid-February. On the 55th day, the near term downtrend continued after a period of consolidation in March. Therefore, the next Fibonacci Time Zone day could be of significance. It is the 89th day, and it will occur at around mid-May. Investors should therefore be prepared for outsized price moment and volatility around that timeframe.

Projection

Using the early December peak, the late December low, and the retracement to 300 as the inputs, we can see the Fibonacci Projection's outlook for the stock. The 100% level acted as support for MDB back in early March but became a bit of resistance as an early April bounce was unable to break above this line. Moving forward, the stock may experience difficulty breaking past this point. As for support, MDB may have found some support at the 127.2% level. The bullish engulfing pattern occurred at this level, and note that it also coincides with the 150 round number in the stock. With multiple technical reasons for the high 140s to low 150s range being support, this level could be an important floor moving forward.

Ichimoku Cloud (Advanced)

The stock's bearish deviation with the cloud is very large currently, showing that general bearishness has been considerable. The cloud itself is bearish as the Leading Span A had a bearish crossover with the Leading Span B back in early February. The cloud isn't very thick as of now and even moving into mid May and so at least the resistance may not be overly strong. With the Leading Span A continuing to fall, expect the cloud to pressure the stock nonetheless. The Conversion Line spent a short period above the Base Line for the month of February but ultimately, it broke back below the Base Line to show a resurgence in near term bearish momentum. Lastly, the Lagging Span is currently very far from late February levels in the stock and so there is confirmation of the downtrend in MDB. There was a key sell signal back in early March during the major gap down. The stock fell far beneath the cloud, the cloud was bearish, the Conversion and Base Lines had a bearish crossover, and the Lagging Span plunged beneath the stock price of early January.

Fundamental Analysis

Earnings

Quarterly Results

MDB released their FY2025 Q4 earnings back in early March and showed mixed results as a whole. The company posted revenues of $548.4 million, up 20% YoY while adjusted EPS was reported to be $1.28, up 48.8% YoY. Both figures beat analyst expectations as revenue and EPS beat by $28.56 million and $0.61 respectively. On the other hand, cash flow from operations results were a bit worrying. They generated $50.5 million versus the prior year period's $54.6 million, and so there seems to be some weakness in the business. As shown in the above chart, revenue growth had QoQ declines as the long term downtrend in the figure continues. Revenue growth is still very near three year lows and in fact Q4 showed the second worst result of the past three years. For cash from operations, I believe it is clear from the chart above that growth has been sluggish at best over the past few years. Overall, earnings were satisfactory but there are reasons to be cautious in my view. Poor revenue growth and declines in cash from operations are potentially ominous signs for the business.

Guidance

MDB provided a FY2026 Q1 outlook as part of the earnings release. They are expecting revenues to be in the range of $524 million to $529 million. Revenue back in FY2025 Q1 was $450.6 million, and so the implied growth rate at the midpoint is 16.8%. That is a further slowdown from Q4's 19.7% YoY increase. For EPS, they are projecting an adjusted figure of between $0.63 and $0.67. Adjusted EPS for FY2025 Q1 was $0.51 and so this guidance represents growth of 27.5% at the midpoint. This is a major slowdown from Q4's 48.8% growth rate. In my view, these guidance figures add to the concern that the business is experiencing difficulty. Furthermore, Seeking Alpha has a D Revisions Grade for MDB currently, as all thirty FY1 Revisions in the past 90 days have been downward. Therefore, the outlook seems bleak at this point.

Valuation

Price To Sales Ratio [P/S]

MDB seems to be relatively cheap according to the P/S ratio's history but versus the Information Technology sector, it remains significantly overvalued. This may suggest chronic overvaluation in the stock. As shown above, the P/S ratio has contracted to three-year lows and is currently at a reading of 6.042. As discussed above, revenue growth is sluggish, but it is holding above FY2025 Q2 levels of growth. In their Q1 guidance, they project that growth will deteriorate further but as you can see on the revenue growth chart above, the expected 16.8% YoY increase would still hold above FY2025 Q2 low. Therefore, the P/S ratio's contraction to levels far below mid-calendar 2024 seems to be an overreaction. That could mean that MDB is undervalued versus how the company has been valued historically. However, the IT sector's median P/S ratio only sits at 2.51 currently, and so the stock is still at a 140.7% premium to peers. I believe this is hard to justify as growth has been sluggish and is expected to continue to deteriorate into their Q1. Therefore, as a whole, I conclude that MDB is overvalued currently and likely has been chronically overvalued in the past few years.

Conclusion

I made the correct call by putting a sell rating on MDB back in February, and I'm reiterating my view here in this article. In the above analysis, the technicals were shown to be bearish as a whole. There were some positives signs here and there, but overall, weakness is likely to continue. As for the fundamentals, most recent earnings were mixed, but there are concerning results in key figures. Furthermore, Q1 guidance shows a deterioration in revenue growth. For the valuation, the P/S ratio seems to show an overdone contraction, but when you compare the multiple to the IT sector's median, I believe it is clear that the stock remains overvalued at current levels. As a result, I reiterate my sell rating on MDB, as investors can expect both technical and fundamental downside from here.

data.title: Dollar General: Pressure On Both Top Line And Bottom Line (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Summary

I gave a hold rating to Dollar General Corp. (NYSE:DG) in December, with my key thesis being that the stock price is unlikely to move up if SSSG (same-store-sales growth) and EBIT margin remain poor. I downgraded my rating from hold to sell. With macro conditions now deteriorating further—especially following the tariff announcements—I believe both demand and margin pressure will intensify. The consumer backdrop has clearly worsened, and as such, I am downgrading my rating from hold to sell.

This macro environment is not good even for DG.

DG should be one of the companies that benefit from a poor macro environment where consumers trade down and look for value purchases. However, this benefit only surfaces when the macro backdrop is at risk of going into a recession—which I think is where we are today. Back in December, we did not know the magnitude of tariffs and how they could impact the consumers, but with the 2nd of April, I think the situation has changed dramatically. While the 9th April announcement was a relatively positive one, I don’t think it has really improved the state of things.

Remember that China is the US's largest trading partner, and a sizeable mix of the US's consumer product goods are made and imported from China. While DG noted in the recent 10K filing that they only directly imported ~4% of their purchases in 2024 (which likely means they are not impacted by the increase in cost), the broader impact from these tariffs is that they weigh on consumers’ spending budget (we can infer this from the consumer confidence index). And this directly impacts demand for DG, especially in its discretionary category.

The numbers speak for themselves. Firstly, traffic growth contribution to SSSG turned negative for the first time since 2Q23 (down 1.1% y/y in 4Q24). Secondly, in 4Q24, while consumables grew 5.3% y/y, when you look at the discretionary categories, performance is far less encouraging. Seasonal grew just 1% y/y, home products rose 2% y/y, and basic apparel grew 1.6% y/y. Importantly, while this is positive growth, it was mostly due to an easy comp in 4Q23. On a two-year stack basis, all of these categories have seen decelerating y/y growth (seasonal down 6.4%, home products down 9.7%, and basic clothing down 6.3%). This clearly reflects constrained consumer budgets. A good way to frame up the impact on the SSSG outlook is that if SSSG was 1.2% in 4Q24 despite a relatively better macro situation, now that the macro economy is dramatically worse, I think it will be a lot harder to sustain the same level of SSSG.

I am not a macro expert, but if the macroeconomy continues to move in the current direction, I think DG is going to see a lot of demand headwinds that will not make the headlines pretty.

Earnings to see pressure

I believe DG’s SG&A expenses are going to be a much bigger problem if my view on the topline materializes. As of 4Q24, SG&A is 26.5% of sales, a big step up of 294 bps from 23.6% in 4Q23. While this includes $214 million of one-time impact from impairment charges related to store closures, even after adjusting for that, the core figure still went up 86 bps. Notably, this trend has persisted for the past 8 quarters, with absolute SG&A dollars growing by an average of 10% y/y. If DG were able to accelerate the top line, this would not be an issue, but if cost is going up and revenue is coming down, this is a recipe for margins/earnings to collapse.

Although management guided for FY25 EPS to come in between $5.1 and $5.8, which, on paper, sounds positive since it implies positive y/y growth at the midpoint, I think this is a very optimistic guide. Note that the management guide explicitly excludes potential downsides from tariff increases, and since the guide was provided on 13th March, it means that the entire additional 125% tariff on China is not included in the guide. With a consumer already very sensitive to prices, I am inclined to believe this substantial hike will heavily impact consumers’ willingness to spend. As such, I see a high risk of DG missing guidance.

Mentioned in the 4Q24 earnings call: There’s a couple of things that are not contemplated in our guidance. One would be any changes to the tariff expectations from what we maybe don't know as of today. We also don't specifically contemplate any changes to SNAP or other benefit programs in our guidance. And then the other thing would be any significant impact to consumer demand brought on by those impacts of tariffs has not been contemplated in the guidance as well.

Valuation

Given the way the macro environment has progressed, I struggle to understand why DG's share price went up over the past few months (from low of $66 in mid-Jan to ~$90 today. Valuation has also seen a sharp increase from 12x to 15x forward PE. I don’t think these moves are justified, as DG is at risk of seeing demand fall off the cliff and margins compress further, making it highly likely to miss its EPS guidance—which consensus is anchoring their estimates on. If I am right, I expect DG to miss earnings, which will trigger consensus to downgrade their earnings estimates, which will lead to valuations coming back down to 12x.

Investment Risk

I may be entirely wrong about how the macro environment will move, especially with Trump's willingness to reverse his initial plans. If that happens, consumers may be more willing to spend, and although a full macro recovery is unlikely in the near term, DG will still benefit. Especially on the discretionary side, consumers that like to purchase discretionary items probably go for value purchases, which DG offers. These will help DG to meet its FY25 EPS guidance.

Conclusion

My negative view on DG is driven by the timing and magnitude of macro deterioration, which, I think, has not been fully appreciated by the market or management’s guidance. The sharp escalation in tariffs, coupled with falling consumer confidence, directly hurt DG’s demand and earnings. With SG&A rising and topline growth at risk, I see little room for upside surprise. If macro weakness persists, I believe DG will miss its FY25 EPS guidance, which will likely lead to a re-rating lower.

data.title: Astera Labs: Buy The Trade War Pullback, Strong Buy
data.description:
Authored by Garvit Bhandari

Recap of previous article on ALAB

The previous article on Astera Labs, Inc. (NASDAQ:ALAB) highlighted the attractiveness of its product portfolio, strong demand from hyperscalers and its upbeat growth prospects given a large and rapidly expanding TAM.

In this follow-up article, I am highlighting a promising 2025 driven by revenue contribution from all four product lines. The company is well positioned for the next phase of growth. I continue to remain bullish and reiterate my Strong Buy rating.

Investment Summary

Astera Labs is a leading provider of semiconductor-based connectivity solutions for cloud and AI infrastructure.

Thanks to its significant investments in the business during 2024, ALAB is well positioned to capitalize on the large market opportunities that lies ahead.

2025 will see revenue contribution from all four product lines and the growth prospects remain bright. The stock has given up all the gains of 2024, and I believe the significant correction offers a compelling entry point for long-term investors. I have a Strong Buy rating and a target price of $124.

Next phase of growth driven by all four product lines

ALAB delivered strong results for FY24. Full-year revenues stood at $396.3 million, a YoY jump of 242%. Moreover, during the year, the company invested significantly in its R&D activities. The overall headcount increased by around 80% to 440 full-time employees.

I strongly believe that ALAB's investments are likely to pay off in 2025, driven by contribution from all of its four product lines. Because, across the industry, hyperscalers are demanding better products that support large language models which continue to grow in complexity and capacity. There is also the fact that hyperscalers have massive capex investments lined up in cloud and AI infrastructure. For instance, ALAB has developed customized AI racks based on third-party GPUs that will be used in datacenters, with its Aries and Scorpio products to maximize GPU throughput and with volume deployments expected in the second half of the year.

In regard to ALAB's product portfolio, I expect its Aries and Taurus product lines to deliver on their strong growth trajectory in FY25. Meanwhile, its Leo product line is likely to witness volume ramp up in the second half of FY25. However, I'm especially enthusiastic about ALAB's Scorpio switches. These switches will begin ramping during the year with new engagements. Given the large market opportunity, Scorpio switches are likely to become the largest of the product lines.

Significant stock price correction provides a compelling entry point

ALAB had seen a stellar run-up during 2024 but has since corrected significantly, giving away all the gains. I believe this significant correction offers a compelling entry opportunity for long-term investors.

ALAB was listed in March 2024 and rose handsomely by ~72% to close at ~$62 on its first trading day. Given its promising AI and cloud applications, the stock zoomed to around ~$147 in December 2024.

However, the stock has given up all its gains in YTD 2025 driven by three reasons: the impact of DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) on AI stocks, its 4Q24 earnings and recent tariff concerns. The stock is currently trading at ~$53 , which is lower than its IPO debut. I believe this significant correction provides a compelling entry point.

Guidance

ALAB has issued the following guidance for 1Q25:

  • Revenues in the range of $151 to $155 million.
  • Non-GAAP gross margins of 74%.
  • Non-GAAP diluted EPS of $0.28 to $0.29.

Financial Projections

ALAB delivered an impressive YoY growth of 242% for FY24. Based on its 1Q25 topline guidance of $151-$155 million, I am modelling in around 70% growth for FY25. Moreover, I expect around 30% topline growth during FY26-27 as newer product lines contribute to the overall revenue.

Based on the revenue growth, I expect EBITDA margins to improve from 31% in FY24 to 37% for FY27. On similar lines, I am estimating non-GAAP EPS of $1.43 for FY25 and $2.41 for FY27.

Valuation

For valuation purposes, I'm using a combination of P/S and P/E to arrive at the fair value of ALAB.

I have compared ALAB with its peers for each of its product lines. I value ALAB using target multiples of 18x sales for the P/S method and 50x earnings using P/E. These target multiples are at a significant premium to its peer group. I attribute this premium to its superior revenue growth, ~50% over FY24-26e vs. 23% for the peer group.

The average target price (TP) stands at $124 based on $126 for P/S and $121 using P/E.

Risks

Below are the main risks to my investment thesis:

  • The company has a history of reporting net losses, and it is not clear when it will report profitability.
  • ALAB derives a substantial part of its revenue from a few customers (3 customers account for 71% of total revenue for FY24). As such, there is a customer concentration risk and any loss of any of these customers will have an adverse impact on the company's business.
  • The company has a limited operating history and may not sustain its high growth rate.

Conclusion

ALAB is a leading provider of semiconductor-based connectivity solutions for cloud and AI infrastructure. Over the last year, the company has made significant investments in the business and has seen 80% growth in its employee base. These investments are expected to pay off in 2025 with all four product lines contributing to the topline.

The stock has given up all its gains of 2024, and I believe the correction provides a compelling entry point for long-term investors. I have a Strong Buy rating and a target price of $124.

data.title: MongoDB: Temporary Weakness Has Created An Attractive Entry Point
data.description:
MongoDB's (NASDAQ:MDB) share price has been under pressure in recent months, with investors likely concerned about the company's lack of direct exposure to AI, competition from the likes of PostgreSQL and general macro uncertainty. MongoDB continues to land customers at a healthy pace, though, and AI-driven infrastructure modernization and AI-enabled app modernization could provide growth tailwinds, particularly if the demand environment improves.

I previously suggested that the recent pullback was a buying opportunity. While MongoDB's share price is down around 30% since then, my opinion hasn't changed. MongoDB is a high-quality company operating under challenging conditions. Even if MongoDB remains a somewhat niche solution, there is still potential for double-digit revenue growth over the long-term and likely high margins at scale, which should lead to strong returns.

Market Conditions

MongoDB's business continues to underperform relative to what might be expected based on AWS' growth. This is unsurprising though given MongoDB's lack of direct exposure to AI.

It should also be kept in mind that consumption is tied to the applications built on MongoDB's platform, meaning its business has relatively high exposure to economic conditions. MongoDB's customer base also skews towards SMBs, who have been hard hit by economic conditions in recent years.

Competition from both the hyperscalers and PostgreSQL remain concerns for MongoDB. Adoption of PostgreSQL appears to be fairly rapid at the moment, which MongoDB is attributing to the ease of shifting from Oracle, Sybase, and SQL Server. I question the extent to which MongoDB is competing directly with PostgreSQL, though. Regardless, MongoDB has suggested its win rates against PostgreSQL are high, as are its win rates against the hyperscalers.

The number of job openings mentioning MongoDB in the job requirements points towards stabilization or even a return to growth, rather than ongoing weakness and competitive pressure. While this is a noisy indicator, it aligns with the growth of MongoDB's customer base and the company's commentary.

Even if MongoDB remains a relatively niche solution, I believe the company is well positioned due to the rapid growth of unstructured data. For example, an estimated 80% of the data generated in 2025 will be unstructured, and its growth is expected to significantly outpace that of structured data.

MongoDB Business Updates

While MongoDB expects AI to continue to proliferate, this will be a gradual process, and hence tailwinds in FY26 are likely to be modest. Many customers are still developing the necessary capabilities and there are still issues around things like hallucinations.

MongoDB is trying to help address hallucination concerns with its recent acquisition of Voyage AI. The acquisition provides embedding and reranking models which help to ensure accurate and relevant results. Total consideration was 220 million USD, with 20 million USD of this in cash. Voyage AI will add an immaterial amount of revenue in FY26.

Another focus area for MongoDB is using AI to support application modernization. MongoDB has demonstrated that its services and AI tooling can significantly accelerate modernization efforts and reduce cost. MongoDB is hoping to expand these efforts this year, potentially leading to stronger growth in FY27. This could provide a service revenue boost in the short term, but this work is likely to be performed at a loss. The company will initially target Java apps running on Oracle.

MongoDB also believes that it will benefit from AI applications using its database, although usage of these applications will need to scale first. Thousands of small customers are building AI apps on MongoDB, and several large AI companies are also using MongoDB. Some of these AI applications are reportedly gaining traction, although the revenue likely isn't meaningful for MongoDB yet. Customers are also adopting MongoDB's vector database.

Financial Analysis

MongoDB generated 548 million USD in the fourth quarter, up 20% YoY. Atlas revenue increased 24%, with better than anticipated consumption. Non-Atlas deals were also unexpectedly strong in the quarter, although this strength isn't really indicative of the current state of that part of the business. MongoDB has stated that its non-Atlas ARR growth was in the mid-single digits in Q4, down from low-double digit growth in the prior year comparable period. The decline in growth is likely due to EA customers deploying incremental workloads on Atlas.

MongoDB expects 524-529 million USD revenue in Q1, representing roughly 17% YoY growth at the midpoint. With a typical earnings beat, I believe growth is likely to come in closer to 20%, though. For the full year, growth is expected to be in the low teens, with the non-Atlas business declining as a result of fewer multi-year deals. Atlas consumption growth is expected to be stable in FY26 though, and guidance seems to suggest that Atlas growth will stabilize in the mid to low 20% range.

MongoDB now has 54,500 customers, adding 1,900 customers in the fourth quarter. This growth is fairly strong, given that MongoDB is now focusing on larger customers. Sales force productivity is reportedly improving, and MongoDB's win rates and retention rates are strong. MongoDB's net ARR expansion rate was approximately 118% in Q4, declining modestly sequentially. While this expansion rate is healthy, at some point newer solutions like search, vector search and stream processing will need to provide a boost.

MongoDB's non-GAAP operating income in Q4 was 112 million USD at a 21% margin. Operating cash flow totalled 50 million USD in Q4. CapEx was elevated due to the final purchase of IPv4 addresses, which amounted to 24 million USD.

Non-GAAP income from operations is expected to total 54-58 million USD in Q1. For the full year, non-GAAP income from operations is expected to be 210-230 million USD. This represents roughly a 5% margin decline from the prior year, which MongoDB has suggested will be due to an absence of high margin multi-year license revenue. MongoDB is also increasing its investments in R&D to improve scalability and performance and to add functionality to its database. In addition, the company is increasing its marketing investments to try and drive adoption of its solutions.

While MongoDB's margins are far from impressive at the moment, I believe the company can achieve operating margins of around 30% at maturity. This is largely the result of its relatively high retention rate. Margins of this magnitude lie many years in the future, though.

Conclusion

MongoDB's business has struggled in recent years, which, I believe, is largely the result of a challenging macro environment. While I don't expect this situation to change in the short-term, there are signs that MongoDB's business remains relatively healthy, despite growing concerns about competition. In particular, the success of PostgreSQL rightly raises questions about MongoDB's competitive positioning, although I tend to think it mainly indicates that NoSQL remains a niche solution.

Analysts are expecting MongoDB's revenue growth rate to stabilize in the mid to high teens, which I believe ignores both the headwinds the company has faced in recent years and the tailwinds that could emerge in coming years. MongoDB's expansion and retention rates remain healthy, and the company's customer base continues to grow at a solid pace, despite a shift in focus towards larger customers. The database category has historically been very attractive for market leaders, and MongoDB's margins should continue to improve as its business scales and matures.

While MongoDB has little direct exposure to AI, tailwinds could emerge over the next few years. For example, generative AI increases the value of unstructured data and could force companies to modernize their infrastructure. AI also lowers switching costs, something that MongoDB is leaning into with both products and services.

Even if MongoDB's growth rate doesn't rebound, the company appears attractively priced at the moment. I believe that MongoDB is capable of achieving operating margins in excess of 30% at maturity, which likely means that the company will have a PE ratio of around 13 within the next 5 years, using normalized earnings. MongoDB also has around 2.3 billion USD liquidity and no debt, positioning the company to return large amounts of capital to shareholders as margins move higher.

The company's revenue multiple is now also near all-time lows, although this must be weighed against the fact that growth has also decelerated sharply in recent years. Relative to comparable companies with similar growth rates, MongoDB generally has a much lower revenue multiple, though.

While MongoDB is a high-quality company that is reasonably priced, there are still substantial risks involved with an investment. MongoDB is only marginally profitable and continues to invest aggressively in future growth, creating a risk of larger losses in the event growth comes in weaker than expected. The demand environment could also deteriorate, which would likely drag MongoDB's growth and share price lower.

data.title: Aristotle Atlantic's Core Equity Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Markets Review

The U.S. equity market began 2025 with a modest decline, with the S&P 500 Index (SPX)(SP500) falling 4.27% during the first quarter. In contrast, bonds provided a measure of stability, as the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index rose 2.78%.

On a sector basis, negative performance was led by four of the eleven sectors within the S&P 500 Index in the first quarter of 2025. The weakest sectors were Consumer Discretionary and Information Technology. The best-performing sectors were Energy and Health Care.

U.S. economic data reported during the quarter presented a mixed picture. Real GDP growth slowed to an annualized rate of 2.4%, while inflation remained stable. The Consumer Price Index (CPI) rose 2.8% year-over-year in February, reflecting moderate inflationary pressures. Meanwhile, the labor market remained resilient, with the unemployment rate hovering around 4%. However, consumer strength showed signs of strain, as retail sales slowed from levels seen late last year, potentially impacted by adverse weather and broader macroeconomic uncertainty.

Trade policy was a major source of uncertainty during the quarter. President Trump announced a series of new tariffs on imports from Canada, Mexico and China, citing concerns over illegal immigration, drug trafficking and intellectual property theft. Targeted industries included autos, steel, aluminum and energy, particularly Venezuelan oil. While tariffs initially raised concerns, the administration’s selective enforcement and flexible implementation approach helped ease market anxiety. In response to the evolving economic landscape, the Federal Reserve (Fed) maintained its target range for the federal funds rate at 4.25% to 4.50%. The central bank acknowledged potential inflationary pressures from tariffs and moderated expectations for economic growth in 2025.

Despite broader economic headwinds, corporate earnings remained strong. S&P 500 companies reported impressive 17.8% year-over-year earnings growth, the highest rate since 2021. However, tariff-related uncertainties loomed large, with more than 220 companies referencing tariffs in their earnings calls and nearly 15% issuing negative earnings guidance.

On the domestic front, a government shutdown was averted, as President Trump signed a six-month funding bill. Senate Democratic Leader Chuck Schumer supported the measure, believing that a shutdown would have allowed the Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE) to terminate government services at a faster rate.

Geopolitically, the U.S. continued its mediation efforts in the Middle East and Ukraine. While a temporary ceasefire agreement was reached between Israel and Hamas in January, tensions flared again in March over disputes regarding hostage releases in Gaza. In Ukraine, U.S. aid was briefly paused following a contentious White House meeting between presidents Trump and Zelensky. Financial and intelligence support resumed after Ukraine signaled it was open to a ceasefire and agreed to revisit terms of a potential mineral deal, aiming to offset the costs of U.S. assistance.

Performance and Attribution Summary

For the first quarter of 2025, Aristotle Atlantic’s Core Equity Composite posted a total return of -5.90% gross of fees (-5.99% net of fees), underperforming the S&P 500 Index, which recorded a total return of -4.27%.

Performance (%) 1Q25 1 Year 3 Years 5 Years 10 Years Since Inception*
Core Equity Composite (gross) -5.90 6.76 7.59 17.19 12.58 13.41
Core Equity Composite (net) -5.99 6.32 7.15 16.72 12.10 12.90
S&P 500 Index -4.27 8.25 9.06 18.59 12.10 12.92

During the first quarter, the portfolio’s underperformance relative to the S&P 500 Index was due to a mix of security selection and allocation effects. Security selection in Information Technology and Industrials detracted the most from relative performance; conversely, security selection in Consumer Discretionary and Utilities contributed the most to relative performance.

Contributors and Detractors for 1Q 2025

Relative Contributors Relative Detractors
Intercontinental Exchange (ICE) Broadcom (AVGO)
Guardant Health (GH) ServiceNow (NOW)
O’Reilly Automotive (ORLY) Bio-Techne (TECH)
Antero Resources (AR) Oracle (ORCL)
Vertex Pharmaceuticals (VRTX) Chart Industries (GTLS)

Detractors

Broadcom

Broadcom detracted from performance in the first quarter. The release of the DeepSeek AI model and analysis of technological specifications stunned investors. This raised concerns about a rapid decline in AI model development costs and the effect that will have on capex spending for new infrastructure and advanced semiconductors used for accelerated computing needs. The entire AI infrastructure investment space saw significant declines in the final week of January following the DeepSeek announcements and continued to see further weakness in March as concerns around AI capex cuts increased.

ServiceNow

ServiceNow detracted from performance in the first quarter, as the company reported guidance during the fourth quarter earnings call that was below investor expectations. Investors have also begun to incorporate tougher macroeconomic headwinds and negative impacts from the DOGE government spending cuts into their weaker outlook for software revenue growth in 2025.

Contributors

Intercontinental Exchange

Intercontinental Exchange was a relative contributor in the first quarter following a solid fourth quarter earnings report highlighted by continued strong trading activity in energy and interest rate products, additional efficiency gains, and positive commentary about the Mortgage Technology business. In addition, optimism improved following the company’s annual ICE Experience conference, which highlighted new AI solutions in its Mortgage Technology product suite. The company’s AI efforts in Mortgage Technology are accelerating progress toward improving and digitizing workflows and positioning it to gain an increasing share of the long-term market opportunity in the mortgage industry.

Guardant Health

Guardant Health was a relative contributor in the first quarter following several positive announcements and solid fourth quarter earnings. Guardant announced Advanced Diagnostic Laboratory Test (ADLT) pricing from Medicare on its Guardant Shield test and a contract with the VA hospital system to cover patients over 45 years of age. This follows strong momentum and positive full-year revenue guidance.

Recent Portfolio Activity

The table below shows all buys and sells completed during the quarter, followed by a brief rationale.

Buys Sells
Analog Devices (ADI)

Buys

Analog Devices

Analog Devices is a global semiconductor leader dedicated to solving customers’ most complex engineering challenges. The company delivers innovations that connect technology to human breakthroughs and play a critical role at the intersection of the physical and digital worlds by providing the building blocks to sense, measure, interpret, connect and power. Analog designs, manufactures, tests and markets a broad portfolio of solutions. These solutions include integrated circuits, software and subsystems that leverage high-performance analog, mixed-signal and digital signal processing technologies. Its comprehensive product portfolio, deep domain expertise and advanced manufacturing span high-performance precision and high-speed mixed-signal, power management and processing technologies, including data converters, amplifiers, power management, radio frequency, integrated circuits, edge processors and other sensors. The company’s customers include original equipment manufacturers and customers that build electronic subsystems for integration into larger systems.

We see the company’s analog products providing exposure to high-growth trends, including automotive electrification and driver assistance systems, factory intelligence and automation, the Intelligent Edge, Internet of Things device proliferation and sustainable energy. We expect the company to return excess free cash flow, benefiting shareholders.

Sells

There were no sales in the first quarter of 2025.

Outlook

The equity markets in the first quarter declined as investors digested the potential impact of wide-ranging tariffs, along with concerns of a peak in the capital spending cycle around AI. Interest rates declined in the quarter, with the 10-year U.S. Treasury yield down about 35 basis points. The prospects of tariffs pushing prices higher could put the Fed on hold in a period when the economy starts to weaken. Economic data was mostly in line with expectations, reflecting a moderately growing economy. Although equity market valuations have pulled back, they are still in elevated territory with growing concerns of a slowdown in corporate profits. Our focus will continue to be at the company level, with an emphasis on seeking to invest in companies with secular tailwinds or strong product-driven cycles.

Index Disclosures

The Russell 1000® Growth Index measures the performance of the large cap growth segment of the U.S. equity universe. It includes those Russell 1000 companies with higher price-to-book ratios and higher forecasted growth values. This index has been selected as the benchmark and is used for comparison purposes only.
